result:
The Light Between Crevices

I woke at four-fifteen this morning because the streetlight outside my window flickered once, then steadied, like a heartbeat resuming after the merest hesitation. That moment—one micro-pause of a sodium lamp—has become the pivot for everything I’m about to set down. It is remarkable how the merest flicker can reset a life that was running in comfortable loops: coffee, desk, errands, occasional rapture at a sunset glimpse through stalled blinds. A single tremor of light, one you didn’t summon or ever asked for, is enough to peel back the wallpaper of habit and reveal the curious plumbing behind it.

What leaked out first was time. I don’t mean the odometer ‘tick’ of clocks, but time as a thick medium, syrupy and multi-layered, so that I could see the houseplant I over-watered last winter, the lemon thyme I allowed to flower for lack of pruning, the tiny ring of calcium on the kettle confirming how many pots of tea I have boiled only half drunk. They all subsisted inside the same moment, which is also the same moment in which my great-grandmother carried a tin kettle across a frozen river during the winter of 1939 and the one where my neighbor’s Labrador sniffs a discarded chicken bone at the end of the block. Synchrony is only distance misread; every hour is mashed atop every other hour, accordion folds of sensation.

I got up and poured a glass of water, my bare feet relieved that I hadn’t put last night’s boots away because their sheepskin cuffs held the day’s residual warmth, a kindness the tiled floor withheld. Somewhere in that crossing—from bed to sink—I recognized I was halfway into an unmarked corridor. The kitchen, for instance, had extended. The dimness at the far end announced itself as a month in October I once spent in Patrick Leigh Fermor’s Mani. I’d never been to the Mani, but now its limestone terraces, its thyme-needled air, were boulevarded next to the spice rack. My passport corroborated no Greek stamps, yet the calendar sagging on the cabinet insisted that sixteen days from tomorrow I had ferried south from Kalamata and hiked past tower houses toward Cape Tenaro. Morning bread smells braided with arid karst and early shore wind—the entire bouquet unfurled from the coffee tin I finally opened.

And I wasn’t dreaming; rather, I had joined the ongoing dream that the house itself is having. Houses dream more slowly than inhabitants, once every thirty to forty years, and the dream recalibrates wall studs and copper pipes until former tenants and future ones can converse midway in architecture. The electrician who upgraded our circuit in 1997 had stubbed his cigarette into the soil of the front potting bench; today its orange glow re-enters the narrative like the lithograph of an ancient hearth seen through a microscope.

I decided not to fight the drift. Instead I toasted yesterday’s bread over the gas burner, holding it with chopsticks because the toaster objected to growing old and jammed months ago. While char smoked upward, I noticed the flame’s color grading from cobalt at its base to a thready cusp of saffron—the precise degradation found in that Hiroshige woodblock of a cock crowing beneath an early sun. The print always hangs in a museum two blocks away; now it reprinted itself on the transient canvas of living fire. Art prefers temporary visas. Photographs learned that the hard way—no still image ever survives its own stillness without recalcitrant time raveling its edges. So I flipped the bread, letting one mythology burnish another.

I sat at the table, ate the toast unbuttered, and counted heartbeats until each became a nurse or a Persian minaret or a child holding a blue balloon in 1986. When there was nothing left to eat, the dish refused to stay empty. It became a reflecting pool full of subwoofer pulses from the nightclub three doors down, boreal greens and rabbit-ear antennas of earlier decades jittering across black water. It was history doing karaoke to the latest bassline. I put my wrist into the pool and felt the DJ’s fingers—three rings of neon—scratch vinyl grooves on my radial artery. At 110 bpm my childhood goldfish reenacted its own small death spiral. I pulled my hand back before the fish reached terminal shimmer.

The saucepan on the stovetop began resonating, producing an arpeggio pure enough to make my collarbones ache. It was calling the cilantro that languished in the crisper. I rescued the herb and laid its bouquet on the cutting board: crushed stems smelled of rain on copper domes in Oaxaca. The knife vibrated like a tuning fork—indeed every metal in the room had become an Aeolian harp, the refrigerator coils, the brass faucet; they hummed a collective major seventh that approximated the note I once accidentally discovered on a broken pump organ in a Wyoming thrift store. I chopped, not to cook, but to accompany the music. Bits of cilantro fell like tiny green meteors. Somewhere a marimba player in a garden three summers ago looked up, heard his own past motif returned to sender, and began to improvise a countermelody. Onions joined the polyphony, converted into transverse flutes via the precise angle of my knife, so that Chopin’s “Raindrop” Prelude dripped into guacamole no one would taste.

I had not left the kitchen for over an hour yet circled three continents at walking speed. One circuit of the sink equaled midsummer night in Vienna where the courtyard well hosted fireflies trained by Maria Theresa’s court musicians. Another orbit of the fridge lands me in my former in-laws’ pantry the Christmas Eve they served oysters chilled on corrugated iron laid across driftwood snow. My tongue re-salted itself from seawater I never drank. The jar of miso opened its label and revealed cave paintings, bison sketched in red ochre forty thousand years older than soy fermentation. I sealed it again; some stories ferment best in darkness.

From the pantry I solved gravity. The tin of sardines had printed across its underside, in raised braille, the statement: “Weight is simply nostalgia masquerading as physics.” When I pressed a finger to the dots, the equation expanded—mass equals the aggregate memory of every love letter never sent, divided by the lingering scent of garlic on fingertips. The sardines themselves were an anthology of boreal terns, plankton shimmer, and the Portuguese fishermen’s lullabies; their oil held moonlit phosphorescence harvested from the night I proposed to no one under a dock outside Lisbon. I ate the nostalgia neatly on rye. It clung to the roof of my mouth like a ballad in a minor key.

The hallway gonged midnight though sunrise was still two hours away; the clock had slipped paces too, hopping an octave. I stopped correcting appliances. I had been barefoot so long the soles of my feet grew translucent and I counted, beneath the skin, the trabecular lattice of the house’s long bones. Each toe had become an antenna: the right pinky tapped into the copper brew of domestic wiring; the left big toe translated between latitudes. Sidewalk cracks gossiped in Morse. Somewhere below the parquet a subway line we’d petitioned against in 2003 tunneled anyway, its ghost train still running, conductors reading aloud the tenant complaints that once fluttered into their suggestion box. I joined them mid-sentence.

“Four fifteen,” I said to the empty platform. The echoes repeated it in four languages and a loon call.

There were other travelers waiting, some from centuries far more courteous than ours. A woman in a plumed hat asked if I carried letters for Bergen-op-Zoom, 1672. I showed her my phone’s lock screen—an indigo bunting—which she accepted as salutation enough; birds, to a Stuart-era mind, already signify. A dervish in teal side-whiskers spun slowly, distributing origami fortunes. Mine unfolded into a spice market, each fold portending a future Sunday. Then the train arrived, roofed not with corrugated steel but with starlight condensed into vaulted glazing. We boarded without tickets; memory is free if you survive the turnstile of your own pulse.

In the aisle seat a botanist of pre-Cambrian algae slept against the window, breathing out such minty oxygen that envious orchids sprouted from my backpack zipper. The orchids pollinated themselves by employing two passengers’ breath—an Etruscan law clerk and my old therapist, who now conversed fluently thanks to a common language of flowering epiphanies. I offered the orchids drops of bottled water left from a visit last June to a bioluminescent bay; they accepted by changing from violet to lunar white, the color of little moons half-drowned in their own luminescence.

Across the ticking triboluminescent rails we discussed seismographs. “Every city has a heartbeat,” said the clerk, “but only insomniacs hear the arrhythmia.” My therapist countered: “Or rather, every arrhythmia begs a city to develop a new sinuous alley where the pulse can wander.” I noticed we had moved beyond hypothetical isoseismic lines; the train itself had arranged its metallic resonance into anodyne patterns by passing briefly through a hundred successive cathodes which appeared then vanished like laughter recalled. Outside—if outside is a word—geologic folds rippled like tablecloths being shaken. Mountains made beds for one another.

The carriage bent gently until it became an esophagus of light ushering us into braille. We exited at Station Nonname which exists at coordinates that keep shifting lest cartographers grow complacent. From the platform I stepped onto a jetty of stacked novels: each spine a plank labeled in gilded latitude, longitudes bound as footnotes. My footfalls sounded muffled as if libraries wore socks. Between 8°34'E and 18°56'W, a breeze turned pages until the history of rainfall leafed open and water began to narrate its own biography in droplets. The story remembered itself as it told itself; its recollections quenched the orchids still perched at my right ear. I tasted epilogue on my tongue—brackish, tinged with engine oil. All departures are horses galloping off margins.

Beyond the jetty lay an orchard pinned to dusk by enormous safety pins, each glinting like Polaris to orient roaming wolves. I joined them, nominating myself honorary moon. The wolves’ paw pads registered every apple wing-beat as we loped across moss that broadcast cricket sonatas in Dolby 5.1. The wolves told me in glances that they collected names of children who correctly knew the difference between sympathy and empathy. When such children blink, a star tags along. We embedded stars into rind markings on gala apples so future shepherds wouldn’t get lost. Every shepherd eventually renames himself after the sound wind makes through the orchard that trained him.

We paused when the sky flushed epidermal pinkists. (Pinkists? I too distrust neologism before coffee, but language at this hour is mercilessly permissive, like foster parents.) Dawn blushed across wolf fur as if embarrassed to interrupt a dream so participative. I realized the orchard floor was printed with fingerprints of every human who ever pruned a tree in March dusk. The prints overlapped like tree rings studded with ancestral whorls. I saw mine, too, though I have never pruned anything more than rosemary hedges on a balcony. Nonetheless, my phantom thumbs had shaped this grove. Maybe intention grafts itself across decades and haunts botanical wrists until the longing to intervene is answered retroactively. We train fruit, and fruit trains us back.

Somewhere in the middle distance a whetted blade sung in treble clef—an old man sharpening scythes. Grindstone sparks rose, fell into blueberry bottles and became constellations for sale at a roadside stand. I traded one wolf a memory of misplacing my blue passport at Havana airport for three currency-independent stars. We attached them to collar bones of the nearest apple tree; the leaves interpreted starlight into Morse Scots-Gaelic, signing “blue.” The language was soft as rainfall on wool. I absorbed it through nostrils until my lungs misted over like hills at harvest. In a way I became the orchard itself: when I exhaled, September apples swelled. One rolled to my toe; it was warm like a hatching idea, embossed with the profile of José Martí. I bit. The citrus-in-shadow notes introduced themselves with proper Latin names, pectin and pigment narrating how revolutions taste when they cool.

Yet the axe still leaned against morality’s oak, and I knew, as one does, that epiphanies have waistlines the same shape as chores. The train had dissolved the moment I alighted; to return home I would need to borrow part of a story briefer than footsteps but sturdier than excuses. The wolves nudged me toward a hollow trunk containing a single shoe without a mate. Cinderella phenomena notwithstanding, the interior conscience sole listed the next duties: defrost the freezer, apologize to my mother about forgotten birthdays, and send-dry laundry before mildew settles in. Routine—the varnish of the invented day—licked its tiny flame along my ankles, recalling me to the tiled kitchen where half an hour ago—or half a cosmos ago—my feet had recognized their belonging.

The orchard released me with nostalgia’s sticky handshake. I stepped onto a road composed entirely of answering-machine messages. The asphalt voice said, “Please leave your message after the beep…” inside which an immense cathedral reverb engulfed the words, so that Theodore Roethke and Emily Dickinson traded baritone replies along the white dashes. I waited for the tone, unsure what to record. Above, a skein of geese reversed direction mid-V to spell “delete.” The truth is, I had already left fifteen thousand messages without meaning to, in sighs and kitchen chair creaks and library fines. I pressed SEND by walking.

At the city edge a pedestrian bridge arced across dried-up fog as if charting a chord change for an absent guitar. Halfway across, graffiti declared in aerosol constellations: “I was here until the here I was.” I nodded—a datum unto itself—and continued. On the far side, the familiar door to our building, lacquered red, waited without suspicion. I touched handle; it unlocked with skin temperature. The stairwell smelled of tulips left too long in vases, that sweet watery collapse. I climbed toward my apartment—the real one, still happening, still holding breath—passing on the third landing the tenant whose cat is named Procrastination. Procrastination rubbed himself against the banister, then abruptly left to accomplish an errand he had deferred since 1992. Good for him.

Inside, everything was unchanged yet alight with neon hangover from realm transitions. The toast crumbs still sat on the edge of the cutting board but they had learned to spell. They spelled “RELENT.” The cilantro water made Chlorophyll shadow puppets against the fridge door. Two mugs that usually hoard dust now glistened as though rinsed by celestial kindness. I sat in the same chair as yesterday but tomorrow pressed up behind it, warm and papery.

The window was open. Somehow I’d left it open since whatever morning this was. It let in a breeze that smelled, improbably, of snow from a childhood I did not have. Yet the scent inserted cartilage into a memory of sledding down a bike ramp in cowboy boots screaming “blastoff”—an artifact pilfered from adjacent possibilities. Possibility itself is a porous border; stories we never lived keep hollering over the fence until we borrow their lawnmower. Snow dusted across my laptop keyboard, frizzing letters into braille. Words rearranged. “Four fifteen” became “for a tool hive,” so I took the hint. Hive minds offer stray tools.

At the bottom drawer of my desk lay sketches from a period when I drew cloud angles instead of counting sheep. Unfolding them revealed cumulus debates, cirrus footnotes, a Society of Verticality meeting above the Rockies. One drawing showed a cloud shaped exactly like the negative space between my mother’s clasped when rosaries drip from her hands. I taped it over the radiator to heat into weather again. Snowflake by flake, it began raining onto the parquet, softly, acoustically. Each drop crystallized into a small white telegraph key and began tapping abbreviated voyages: dot dot dash, dash dash dot, the whole alphabet of places I failed—yet failed so completely they looped back around into achievement. Maritime failures, thermodynamic failures, amatory failures: all gathered pulp to become origami gulls and lifted off the floor.

That was when the phone rang. I have not owned a landline in years, but this rotary artifact materialized the way odors return from locked attics. I picked up the earpiece heavy as newborn universe. A voice that felt like blue light said, “Confirm arrival.” I answered, “Arrived where?” The voice countered, “Where you never departed.” I considered this logically enough: if journeys are circuits, the entire timeline is a Möbius strip. My coordinates were both the tip of a lightning conductor and its buried root in loam.

“Okay,” I relayed. “Confirmed. Now what?”

The line crackled like a hearth digesting syllables. Then came a long oceanic inhale followed by exhale as ordinary as evening dishes. I hung up because the conversation had concluded. Outside the window the streetlight stuttered again—three deliberate flashes and one tremulous final glow—then folded its orange iris as if tucking the city into a bedsheet. With the light gone I could see that the constellation Orion had slipped lower, westering above buildings while a smudge of pre-sun lit the clouds charcoal rose. Four fifty-two. Morning was no longer hypothetical.

I wanted to catalogue everything, but taxonomies demagnetize wonder. Instead I left a breadcrumb of syllables on my forearm with dry-erase marker: “House breathes stop.” Enough. I brewed coffee. The grinder gossiped about Ethiopian hillsides until beans surrendered their somber past. While water heated, I dialed air’s FM stations. Frequency 88.9 hosted a program named “Flora of Forgotten Stations”; the host spoke only in species binomials: Lathyrus odoratus, Verbena bonariensis, Amaranthus caudatus. The list formed a litany so precisely weighted that when the kettle clicked off, the announcement felt like a blessing over real grounds.

First sip: heaven tastes exactly of cardboard and possibility—because possibility is ninety-eight percent expectation and two percent compound interest on patterns. Second sip: the flavor stepped sideways into apricot pit, which is grief caramelized under high pressure. I accepted the grief, grateful for instruction. We are always a flotilla of certainties lashed together by the finest twine of doubt; cut and we drift, burn, or perform impossible cartwheels mid-air. I carried my misery out onto the balcony before it hardened. There, the neighbor cat had arranged herself symmetrically across the railing as if blueprinted by Japanese paper size. She blinked slow; I blinked slower. Between blinks a whole succession of apartment blocks aged and shed concrete like dandruff. Somewhere acrylic paints peeled off rooftops in strips that fluttered down as urban butterflies. Fact and metaphor swapped ID badges at the door.

Morning’s first mechanical bus exhaled diesel like a dragon that gave up firebreathing for punctuation. Its roar ended in an ellipsis that trailed into pigeon wingspans. I noticed now that all ordinary noises—garbage truck hydraulic whine, skateboarder grinding curb, electrical hum from the utility pole—overlapped into an accidental fugue. I conducted from the balcony rail, waving a teaspoon as baton. Downstairs the postal worker executed an accidental diminuendo when the ambulance siren entered the key of E minor the entire quarter unison. Pedestrians unknowingly sang measures as they crossed the zebra stripes, the pattern its own staff lines. The city arrived at polyphonic consciousness without purchasing a single philosophy textbook.

My coffee cup emptied at the same tempo as sunrise filling the sky; one vacuum replaced another. I rinsed the cup only to discover it had reproduced: arrays of identical cups aligned inside the sink like mirrored hallways. Careful not to disturb their self-replicating geometry, I pulled one out by the handle called ‘now.’ It remained singular but carried a faint afterglow of multiplication.

Back at the desk, I tried to write what you are reading now. However words refused linear custody. They parachuted onto the page in bouquets, demanding equal size fonts. I negotiated. Eventually we agreed to phrases that commute on bicycles instead of verdicts that colonize entire judicial paragraphs. Whenever a period threatened, it was arrested by an em dash plotting escape to another continent. Semicolons masqueraded binoculars surveying later clauses for asylum. Grammar developed stage fright at the idea of imperatives. Imperatives, for their part, stepped down from balconies into geraniums and promised gentleness. Thus the sentence you inhabit now: it has relinquished militancy; it hopes to host your pulse instead.

Yes, this disquisition could become memoir: here is where childhood disappointment leeched into façades of tolerance. It could foreground trauma: here is when the streetlight entered my prefrontal cortex like an abacus telling loss in negative beads. Memoir and trauma are valid cartographers. But today refuses shoreline surveys, insists on surf itself. The very molecules I breathe have passed through whale lungs, engine blocks, epistolary affairs, and geranium pores; to isolate any lineage is short-sighted. Democracy of particulates insists on partnership. Thus this text scrolls horizontally at times, vertically at others, then folds origami-style into a fortune cookie: Crack it and receive a slip that reads, “Light is a polite thief—give up trying to inventory pockets after visits.”

Now—if “now” still consents to ex-ist—my mother phones. She asks whether I’ve remembered to renew my license tabs. Of course, the real mother is continents and months away; this is her quantum voicemail packet traveling along hypotenuse desire. I tell her, “The tabs dissolved into bus transfer slips at the station named Nonname.” In the pause I hear her ironing shirts on another Tuesday still arriving. She sighs, then laughs because the hiss means we both understand nonverbal physics: love alters but never dissolves, operating on a half-life spanning generations. I promise I’ll get to the DMV even though my car was last seen crossing the Sea of Azov with two troubadours and a crate of bartered saffron. Plausible deniability is renewable annually in most states.

After hanging up, I discover the red door of the apartment has become slightly ajar. Through the gap extends an alleyway I did not know was mine: wooden fences advertise positions for vacancy, ivy curls into Morse repeating ‘RENT/LOVE.’ There is mail hung by clothespins to a line strung between bicycles. An envelope addressed to “Occupant: Future Former” contains only a pressed violet and a train schedule from 1903 Tsarist Russia. I pocket both. The alley smells of turpentine and rain on warm concrete; somewhere close a painter rewinds sunset hues across canvas. His footfall echoes in acrylic boots of burnt umber. I borrow cadmium yellow from the air above us and smear it on my left cheek for solidity.

When I loop back through the doorway the threshold has narrowed to a stanza, its lintel inscribed with:

Between what you meant to say
and what silence keeps
grows the constellation of choice.

I duck to spare the font choice migraine.

By late morning I was starving. The kitchen had rebooted to baseline. No more raining keyboards or choral brooms. Beans on toast would suffice. I simmered pinto beans, tasting cumin and smoke the way time indexes memory inside legumes. They sang small calorie hymns in a minor pentatonic scale. As I ate, every bite slipped a metaphor onto the table: this spoon was the moon raking tides; those beans the recalcitrant minutes trying to coagulate. I took memos, chewing epiphany. Hunger quieted into satiety, which is just potential energy resting before its gymnastic reincarnation.

Then came afternoon—that amnesiac raconteur. Afternoon does not believe in its own existence until it has been archived by coffee and pigeons and laundromat cyclic dryers. I walked to the corner store and in doing so re-remembered that even sidewalks gossip in syllables of gum. Each old stain memorializes a mouth that chewed expectancy until brittle, then ejected expectation onto progress. City planners call it bio-residue. Poets call it longitude of longing indulged. I call it Tuesday residue painted flat by feet whose soles never noticed they were canvases.

At the store I bought bread thresholded through kneading histories—some wheat farmer in Saskatchewan also named Matthew now co-signs my purchase, though we’ve never met. Receipts always enlist strangers as witnesses. Three limes for two dollars carry the equatorial puckered grin of seven moons ago when the lime merchant’s daughter skipped stones across the irrigation canal en route to escape village drought. I added tomatoes whose redness once ripened at a traffic light stuck on stop. The cashier’s hands reminded me of winters spent shucking corn on grandma’s porch; I paid mostly in stories, coins secondary. Somewhere her scanner pinged a barcode sequence that translated into an aria from Tosca. The whole check-out line held its communal breath until C-minor resolved.

Returning home, I passed construction workers jackhammering rainbows straight through asphalt. They rotated palette wheel drills, shard storms turned prismatic. I nearly joined them, offering lime wedges to lubricate sky-bits, but realized I’d forgotten eggs. Another errand. On the detour I noticed a boy blowing bubbles from a want ad. Each bubble revealed prospective apartments available within bubble lifespan: three-to-four second leases expanded interior fantasies, collapsed, left vacancies for birds. In one popped bubble I registered my own studio reflected inverted—ceilings hosting furniture, teacups nesting on floors. The inversion felt truer than truth. Vertigo fizzed up through ankles, but delight fizzed higher.

I arrived back at four thirty. Dusk enrolled colors with greater diplomacy than I could muster after social exertions. My chairs welcomed my hips like accustomed museums welcome frequent visitors. I settled to disassemble a lamp whose anxiety bulb had blinked. Unscrewing the globe disclosed mothscale galaxies that transformed kitchen dark into nebula soup. I soldered no wires; memory did the conducting. The lamp brightened itself without electricity, proof that attention can override physics when attention chooses. Light pooled amber across dictionary pages I’d torn out years back during fits of definition divorces. Now, corralled by lamplight, words reunited unprompted: “benison” paired with “undone,” “microcosm” learned salsa with “macrocarpa.” They twirled across the floor in shoals like silverfish. I watched without referee: language deserves consensual choreography.

At last evening demanded accounting. I recognized my two feet still anchored to same kitchen where sodium streetlight first derailed default daylight. Yet they were rinsed in star-orchard light now, mapped with alleycat tobacco laughs and Russian violet fragrance and quartz dust from the train that never arrives timely according to maps. I walked barefoot to the stove, heated milk, misread desire for hot chocolate but allowed hope to override and thus invented midnight pudding from powdered starlight and lactose. It tasted sequentially of cinnamon, contrails, cardboard confession boxes, then finally of the cocoa I initially lacked. Absence was merely flavor relocation.

I carried the pudding to balcony just as visible stars peeked through city haze. Somewhere the wolves I’d trekked with paced terraces across the constellations, seeking familiar smells. I clicked tongue thrice—wolf dialect for keep walking. They signed back via constellation twinkles: — — — / · — · good dream realized. I measured my heartbeat against theirs; 64 bpm averaged across interstellar void. While spooning pudding, I recounted to them (knowing sound travels slowly across light-years) the news: alarms silenced, orchards safe, receipts honored, beans digested, lamplight consensual. I could not see if they nodded—their silhouettes subtended by about three degrees of arc—but a meteor shower shot across Cygnus in approval. One meteor carved an ampersand between hearts distant by heaven’s standard accounting, so maybe constellations are also grammarians of implied connection.

Between the rails of the meteor’s remaining stardust I wrote an unwritten letter that begins, ends, and resolves inside this very moment—with no postal price required except attention paid in full. To whom it may concern: The night folds inside your eyelid the way umbrellas fold; when opened skyward, entire symphonies of rain shower merely ourselves disguised. The message is happy to wait in marrow closets until you too wake at four fifteen by a flickering bulb orchestrating time into cathedral choirs.

Somewhere the sodium lamp exhaled a final flare, then ceded authority to planetand then orbit, and my pupils adjusted to receive the gradual gift of blue. The light had stolen nothing; it traded transparency for transparency, ushering inside what was already indoors: the interlaced epochs simmering in teakettle memory, the postal catastrophes arriving disguised as seasons, the child still holding the same blue balloon except now the balloon englobes the Milky Way while the child remains as bright and unsure as ever. I tiptoed back to bed, careful not to disturb my own footprints still spinning freshly minted pentimenti across the floorboards.

As sleep approached, the walls resumed their normal thickness. Yet a slender thread of orchard dusk looped round my finger, gentle reminder that thickness is a courtesy, not a verdict. Whether I remember tomorrow’s physical to-do list—license tabs, defrosted meat, email backlog—matters less than remembering the thread. It will tug whenever I risk defaulting to prepackaged linearity. I rolled onto my side. The mattress took custody of breathing context. Beyond, city transistor radios switched to predawn traffic. Out there, strangers negotiated their own retrievals from lost and found cosmos. I slept. The house exhaled a single copious sigh in relief that its dreamer had re-merged with circadian consensus. Streetlight blinked one last off-duty flicker—half wink, half benison—then recycled itself into elsewhere diodes.

And if you’ve read this far through the spirals and sine waves of episode, welcome to whatever section of night is yours: dawn in some archipelago, dusk in another. The particles stroking your retina once dwelled inside supernova lungs and poeticilium fields. They have taken roadside detours via dreams, detours whose mileage is tallied only by patience. Carry whichever scraps resonate—only remember to lie gently to gravity when you answer, “I’m staying put.” Because you’re moving on tectonic poetry plates at three centimeters a year—hardly noticeable until continents kiss. Until then, keep an ember of orchard warmth on the inside of your eyelids. May whatever house dreams you tonight compost sour old narratives into fertile loam for tentative, looping, luminous tomorrow.

message.content:
The Light Between Crevices

I woke at four-fifteen this morning because the streetlight outside my window flickered once, then steadied, like a heartbeat resuming after the merest hesitation. That moment—one micro-pause of a sodium lamp—has become the pivot for everything I’m about to set down. It is remarkable how the merest flicker can reset a life that was running in comfortable loops: coffee, desk, errands, occasional rapture at a sunset glimpse through stalled blinds. A single tremor of light, one you didn’t summon or ever asked for, is enough to peel back the wallpaper of habit and reveal the curious plumbing behind it.

What leaked out first was time. I don’t mean the odometer ‘tick’ of clocks, but time as a thick medium, syrupy and multi-layered, so that I could see the houseplant I over-watered last winter, the lemon thyme I allowed to flower for lack of pruning, the tiny ring of calcium on the kettle confirming how many pots of tea I have boiled only half drunk. They all subsisted inside the same moment, which is also the same moment in which my great-grandmother carried a tin kettle across a frozen river during the winter of 1939 and the one where my neighbor’s Labrador sniffs a discarded chicken bone at the end of the block. Synchrony is only distance misread; every hour is mashed atop every other hour, accordion folds of sensation.

I got up and poured a glass of water, my bare feet relieved that I hadn’t put last night’s boots away because their sheepskin cuffs held the day’s residual warmth, a kindness the tiled floor withheld. Somewhere in that crossing—from bed to sink—I recognized I was halfway into an unmarked corridor. The kitchen, for instance, had extended. The dimness at the far end announced itself as a month in October I once spent in Patrick Leigh Fermor’s Mani. I’d never been to the Mani, but now its limestone terraces, its thyme-needled air, were boulevarded next to the spice rack. My passport corroborated no Greek stamps, yet the calendar sagging on the cabinet insisted that sixteen days from tomorrow I had ferried south from Kalamata and hiked past tower houses toward Cape Tenaro. Morning bread smells braided with arid karst and early shore wind—the entire bouquet unfurled from the coffee tin I finally opened.

And I wasn’t dreaming; rather, I had joined the ongoing dream that the house itself is having. Houses dream more slowly than inhabitants, once every thirty to forty years, and the dream recalibrates wall studs and copper pipes until former tenants and future ones can converse midway in architecture. The electrician who upgraded our circuit in 1997 had stubbed his cigarette into the soil of the front potting bench; today its orange glow re-enters the narrative like the lithograph of an ancient hearth seen through a microscope.

I decided not to fight the drift. Instead I toasted yesterday’s bread over the gas burner, holding it with chopsticks because the toaster objected to growing old and jammed months ago. While char smoked upward, I noticed the flame’s color grading from cobalt at its base to a thready cusp of saffron—the precise degradation found in that Hiroshige woodblock of a cock crowing beneath an early sun. The print always hangs in a museum two blocks away; now it reprinted itself on the transient canvas of living fire. Art prefers temporary visas. Photographs learned that the hard way—no still image ever survives its own stillness without recalcitrant time raveling its edges. So I flipped the bread, letting one mythology burnish another.

I sat at the table, ate the toast unbuttered, and counted heartbeats until each became a nurse or a Persian minaret or a child holding a blue balloon in 1986. When there was nothing left to eat, the dish refused to stay empty. It became a reflecting pool full of subwoofer pulses from the nightclub three doors down, boreal greens and rabbit-ear antennas of earlier decades jittering across black water. It was history doing karaoke to the latest bassline. I put my wrist into the pool and felt the DJ’s fingers—three rings of neon—scratch vinyl grooves on my radial artery. At 110 bpm my childhood goldfish reenacted its own small death spiral. I pulled my hand back before the fish reached terminal shimmer.

The saucepan on the stovetop began resonating, producing an arpeggio pure enough to make my collarbones ache. It was calling the cilantro that languished in the crisper. I rescued the herb and laid its bouquet on the cutting board: crushed stems smelled of rain on copper domes in Oaxaca. The knife vibrated like a tuning fork—indeed every metal in the room had become an Aeolian harp, the refrigerator coils, the brass faucet; they hummed a collective major seventh that approximated the note I once accidentally discovered on a broken pump organ in a Wyoming thrift store. I chopped, not to cook, but to accompany the music. Bits of cilantro fell like tiny green meteors. Somewhere a marimba player in a garden three summers ago looked up, heard his own past motif returned to sender, and began to improvise a countermelody. Onions joined the polyphony, converted into transverse flutes via the precise angle of my knife, so that Chopin’s “Raindrop” Prelude dripped into guacamole no one would taste.

I had not left the kitchen for over an hour yet circled three continents at walking speed. One circuit of the sink equaled midsummer night in Vienna where the courtyard well hosted fireflies trained by Maria Theresa’s court musicians. Another orbit of the fridge lands me in my former in-laws’ pantry the Christmas Eve they served oysters chilled on corrugated iron laid across driftwood snow. My tongue re-salted itself from seawater I never drank. The jar of miso opened its label and revealed cave paintings, bison sketched in red ochre forty thousand years older than soy fermentation. I sealed it again; some stories ferment best in darkness.

From the pantry I solved gravity. The tin of sardines had printed across its underside, in raised braille, the statement: “Weight is simply nostalgia masquerading as physics.” When I pressed a finger to the dots, the equation expanded—mass equals the aggregate memory of every love letter never sent, divided by the lingering scent of garlic on fingertips. The sardines themselves were an anthology of boreal terns, plankton shimmer, and the Portuguese fishermen’s lullabies; their oil held moonlit phosphorescence harvested from the night I proposed to no one under a dock outside Lisbon. I ate the nostalgia neatly on rye. It clung to the roof of my mouth like a ballad in a minor key.

The hallway gonged midnight though sunrise was still two hours away; the clock had slipped paces too, hopping an octave. I stopped correcting appliances. I had been barefoot so long the soles of my feet grew translucent and I counted, beneath the skin, the trabecular lattice of the house’s long bones. Each toe had become an antenna: the right pinky tapped into the copper brew of domestic wiring; the left big toe translated between latitudes. Sidewalk cracks gossiped in Morse. Somewhere below the parquet a subway line we’d petitioned against in 2003 tunneled anyway, its ghost train still running, conductors reading aloud the tenant complaints that once fluttered into their suggestion box. I joined them mid-sentence.

“Four fifteen,” I said to the empty platform. The echoes repeated it in four languages and a loon call.

There were other travelers waiting, some from centuries far more courteous than ours. A woman in a plumed hat asked if I carried letters for Bergen-op-Zoom, 1672. I showed her my phone’s lock screen—an indigo bunting—which she accepted as salutation enough; birds, to a Stuart-era mind, already signify. A dervish in teal side-whiskers spun slowly, distributing origami fortunes. Mine unfolded into a spice market, each fold portending a future Sunday. Then the train arrived, roofed not with corrugated steel but with starlight condensed into vaulted glazing. We boarded without tickets; memory is free if you survive the turnstile of your own pulse.

In the aisle seat a botanist of pre-Cambrian algae slept against the window, breathing out such minty oxygen that envious orchids sprouted from my backpack zipper. The orchids pollinated themselves by employing two passengers’ breath—an Etruscan law clerk and my old therapist, who now conversed fluently thanks to a common language of flowering epiphanies. I offered the orchids drops of bottled water left from a visit last June to a bioluminescent bay; they accepted by changing from violet to lunar white, the color of little moons half-drowned in their own luminescence.

Across the ticking triboluminescent rails we discussed seismographs. “Every city has a heartbeat,” said the clerk, “but only insomniacs hear the arrhythmia.” My therapist countered: “Or rather, every arrhythmia begs a city to develop a new sinuous alley where the pulse can wander.” I noticed we had moved beyond hypothetical isoseismic lines; the train itself had arranged its metallic resonance into anodyne patterns by passing briefly through a hundred successive cathodes which appeared then vanished like laughter recalled. Outside—if outside is a word—geologic folds rippled like tablecloths being shaken. Mountains made beds for one another.

The carriage bent gently until it became an esophagus of light ushering us into braille. We exited at Station Nonname which exists at coordinates that keep shifting lest cartographers grow complacent. From the platform I stepped onto a jetty of stacked novels: each spine a plank labeled in gilded latitude, longitudes bound as footnotes. My footfalls sounded muffled as if libraries wore socks. Between 8°34'E and 18°56'W, a breeze turned pages until the history of rainfall leafed open and water began to narrate its own biography in droplets. The story remembered itself as it told itself; its recollections quenched the orchids still perched at my right ear. I tasted epilogue on my tongue—brackish, tinged with engine oil. All departures are horses galloping off margins.

Beyond the jetty lay an orchard pinned to dusk by enormous safety pins, each glinting like Polaris to orient roaming wolves. I joined them, nominating myself honorary moon. The wolves’ paw pads registered every apple wing-beat as we loped across moss that broadcast cricket sonatas in Dolby 5.1. The wolves told me in glances that they collected names of children who correctly knew the difference between sympathy and empathy. When such children blink, a star tags along. We embedded stars into rind markings on gala apples so future shepherds wouldn’t get lost. Every shepherd eventually renames himself after the sound wind makes through the orchard that trained him.

We paused when the sky flushed epidermal pinkists. (Pinkists? I too distrust neologism before coffee, but language at this hour is mercilessly permissive, like foster parents.) Dawn blushed across wolf fur as if embarrassed to interrupt a dream so participative. I realized the orchard floor was printed with fingerprints of every human who ever pruned a tree in March dusk. The prints overlapped like tree rings studded with ancestral whorls. I saw mine, too, though I have never pruned anything more than rosemary hedges on a balcony. Nonetheless, my phantom thumbs had shaped this grove. Maybe intention grafts itself across decades and haunts botanical wrists until the longing to intervene is answered retroactively. We train fruit, and fruit trains us back.

Somewhere in the middle distance a whetted blade sung in treble clef—an old man sharpening scythes. Grindstone sparks rose, fell into blueberry bottles and became constellations for sale at a roadside stand. I traded one wolf a memory of misplacing my blue passport at Havana airport for three currency-independent stars. We attached them to collar bones of the nearest apple tree; the leaves interpreted starlight into Morse Scots-Gaelic, signing “blue.” The language was soft as rainfall on wool. I absorbed it through nostrils until my lungs misted over like hills at harvest. In a way I became the orchard itself: when I exhaled, September apples swelled. One rolled to my toe; it was warm like a hatching idea, embossed with the profile of José Martí. I bit. The citrus-in-shadow notes introduced themselves with proper Latin names, pectin and pigment narrating how revolutions taste when they cool.

Yet the axe still leaned against morality’s oak, and I knew, as one does, that epiphanies have waistlines the same shape as chores. The train had dissolved the moment I alighted; to return home I would need to borrow part of a story briefer than footsteps but sturdier than excuses. The wolves nudged me toward a hollow trunk containing a single shoe without a mate. Cinderella phenomena notwithstanding, the interior conscience sole listed the next duties: defrost the freezer, apologize to my mother about forgotten birthdays, and send-dry laundry before mildew settles in. Routine—the varnish of the invented day—licked its tiny flame along my ankles, recalling me to the tiled kitchen where half an hour ago—or half a cosmos ago—my feet had recognized their belonging.

The orchard released me with nostalgia’s sticky handshake. I stepped onto a road composed entirely of answering-machine messages. The asphalt voice said, “Please leave your message after the beep…” inside which an immense cathedral reverb engulfed the words, so that Theodore Roethke and Emily Dickinson traded baritone replies along the white dashes. I waited for the tone, unsure what to record. Above, a skein of geese reversed direction mid-V to spell “delete.” The truth is, I had already left fifteen thousand messages without meaning to, in sighs and kitchen chair creaks and library fines. I pressed SEND by walking.

At the city edge a pedestrian bridge arced across dried-up fog as if charting a chord change for an absent guitar. Halfway across, graffiti declared in aerosol constellations: “I was here until the here I was.” I nodded—a datum unto itself—and continued. On the far side, the familiar door to our building, lacquered red, waited without suspicion. I touched handle; it unlocked with skin temperature. The stairwell smelled of tulips left too long in vases, that sweet watery collapse. I climbed toward my apartment—the real one, still happening, still holding breath—passing on the third landing the tenant whose cat is named Procrastination. Procrastination rubbed himself against the banister, then abruptly left to accomplish an errand he had deferred since 1992. Good for him.

Inside, everything was unchanged yet alight with neon hangover from realm transitions. The toast crumbs still sat on the edge of the cutting board but they had learned to spell. They spelled “RELENT.” The cilantro water made Chlorophyll shadow puppets against the fridge door. Two mugs that usually hoard dust now glistened as though rinsed by celestial kindness. I sat in the same chair as yesterday but tomorrow pressed up behind it, warm and papery.

The window was open. Somehow I’d left it open since whatever morning this was. It let in a breeze that smelled, improbably, of snow from a childhood I did not have. Yet the scent inserted cartilage into a memory of sledding down a bike ramp in cowboy boots screaming “blastoff”—an artifact pilfered from adjacent possibilities. Possibility itself is a porous border; stories we never lived keep hollering over the fence until we borrow their lawnmower. Snow dusted across my laptop keyboard, frizzing letters into braille. Words rearranged. “Four fifteen” became “for a tool hive,” so I took the hint. Hive minds offer stray tools.

At the bottom drawer of my desk lay sketches from a period when I drew cloud angles instead of counting sheep. Unfolding them revealed cumulus debates, cirrus footnotes, a Society of Verticality meeting above the Rockies. One drawing showed a cloud shaped exactly like the negative space between my mother’s clasped when rosaries drip from her hands. I taped it over the radiator to heat into weather again. Snowflake by flake, it began raining onto the parquet, softly, acoustically. Each drop crystallized into a small white telegraph key and began tapping abbreviated voyages: dot dot dash, dash dash dot, the whole alphabet of places I failed—yet failed so completely they looped back around into achievement. Maritime failures, thermodynamic failures, amatory failures: all gathered pulp to become origami gulls and lifted off the floor.

That was when the phone rang. I have not owned a landline in years, but this rotary artifact materialized the way odors return from locked attics. I picked up the earpiece heavy as newborn universe. A voice that felt like blue light said, “Confirm arrival.” I answered, “Arrived where?” The voice countered, “Where you never departed.” I considered this logically enough: if journeys are circuits, the entire timeline is a Möbius strip. My coordinates were both the tip of a lightning conductor and its buried root in loam.

“Okay,” I relayed. “Confirmed. Now what?”

The line crackled like a hearth digesting syllables. Then came a long oceanic inhale followed by exhale as ordinary as evening dishes. I hung up because the conversation had concluded. Outside the window the streetlight stuttered again—three deliberate flashes and one tremulous final glow—then folded its orange iris as if tucking the city into a bedsheet. With the light gone I could see that the constellation Orion had slipped lower, westering above buildings while a smudge of pre-sun lit the clouds charcoal rose. Four fifty-two. Morning was no longer hypothetical.

I wanted to catalogue everything, but taxonomies demagnetize wonder. Instead I left a breadcrumb of syllables on my forearm with dry-erase marker: “House breathes stop.” Enough. I brewed coffee. The grinder gossiped about Ethiopian hillsides until beans surrendered their somber past. While water heated, I dialed air’s FM stations. Frequency 88.9 hosted a program named “Flora of Forgotten Stations”; the host spoke only in species binomials: Lathyrus odoratus, Verbena bonariensis, Amaranthus caudatus. The list formed a litany so precisely weighted that when the kettle clicked off, the announcement felt like a blessing over real grounds.

First sip: heaven tastes exactly of cardboard and possibility—because possibility is ninety-eight percent expectation and two percent compound interest on patterns. Second sip: the flavor stepped sideways into apricot pit, which is grief caramelized under high pressure. I accepted the grief, grateful for instruction. We are always a flotilla of certainties lashed together by the finest twine of doubt; cut and we drift, burn, or perform impossible cartwheels mid-air. I carried my misery out onto the balcony before it hardened. There, the neighbor cat had arranged herself symmetrically across the railing as if blueprinted by Japanese paper size. She blinked slow; I blinked slower. Between blinks a whole succession of apartment blocks aged and shed concrete like dandruff. Somewhere acrylic paints peeled off rooftops in strips that fluttered down as urban butterflies. Fact and metaphor swapped ID badges at the door.

Morning’s first mechanical bus exhaled diesel like a dragon that gave up firebreathing for punctuation. Its roar ended in an ellipsis that trailed into pigeon wingspans. I noticed now that all ordinary noises—garbage truck hydraulic whine, skateboarder grinding curb, electrical hum from the utility pole—overlapped into an accidental fugue. I conducted from the balcony rail, waving a teaspoon as baton. Downstairs the postal worker executed an accidental diminuendo when the ambulance siren entered the key of E minor the entire quarter unison. Pedestrians unknowingly sang measures as they crossed the zebra stripes, the pattern its own staff lines. The city arrived at polyphonic consciousness without purchasing a single philosophy textbook.

My coffee cup emptied at the same tempo as sunrise filling the sky; one vacuum replaced another. I rinsed the cup only to discover it had reproduced: arrays of identical cups aligned inside the sink like mirrored hallways. Careful not to disturb their self-replicating geometry, I pulled one out by the handle called ‘now.’ It remained singular but carried a faint afterglow of multiplication.

Back at the desk, I tried to write what you are reading now. However words refused linear custody. They parachuted onto the page in bouquets, demanding equal size fonts. I negotiated. Eventually we agreed to phrases that commute on bicycles instead of verdicts that colonize entire judicial paragraphs. Whenever a period threatened, it was arrested by an em dash plotting escape to another continent. Semicolons masqueraded binoculars surveying later clauses for asylum. Grammar developed stage fright at the idea of imperatives. Imperatives, for their part, stepped down from balconies into geraniums and promised gentleness. Thus the sentence you inhabit now: it has relinquished militancy; it hopes to host your pulse instead.

Yes, this disquisition could become memoir: here is where childhood disappointment leeched into façades of tolerance. It could foreground trauma: here is when the streetlight entered my prefrontal cortex like an abacus telling loss in negative beads. Memoir and trauma are valid cartographers. But today refuses shoreline surveys, insists on surf itself. The very molecules I breathe have passed through whale lungs, engine blocks, epistolary affairs, and geranium pores; to isolate any lineage is short-sighted. Democracy of particulates insists on partnership. Thus this text scrolls horizontally at times, vertically at others, then folds origami-style into a fortune cookie: Crack it and receive a slip that reads, “Light is a polite thief—give up trying to inventory pockets after visits.”

Now—if “now” still consents to ex-ist—my mother phones. She asks whether I’ve remembered to renew my license tabs. Of course, the real mother is continents and months away; this is her quantum voicemail packet traveling along hypotenuse desire. I tell her, “The tabs dissolved into bus transfer slips at the station named Nonname.” In the pause I hear her ironing shirts on another Tuesday still arriving. She sighs, then laughs because the hiss means we both understand nonverbal physics: love alters but never dissolves, operating on a half-life spanning generations. I promise I’ll get to the DMV even though my car was last seen crossing the Sea of Azov with two troubadours and a crate of bartered saffron. Plausible deniability is renewable annually in most states.

After hanging up, I discover the red door of the apartment has become slightly ajar. Through the gap extends an alleyway I did not know was mine: wooden fences advertise positions for vacancy, ivy curls into Morse repeating ‘RENT/LOVE.’ There is mail hung by clothespins to a line strung between bicycles. An envelope addressed to “Occupant: Future Former” contains only a pressed violet and a train schedule from 1903 Tsarist Russia. I pocket both. The alley smells of turpentine and rain on warm concrete; somewhere close a painter rewinds sunset hues across canvas. His footfall echoes in acrylic boots of burnt umber. I borrow cadmium yellow from the air above us and smear it on my left cheek for solidity.

When I loop back through the doorway the threshold has narrowed to a stanza, its lintel inscribed with:

Between what you meant to say
and what silence keeps
grows the constellation of choice.

I duck to spare the font choice migraine.

By late morning I was starving. The kitchen had rebooted to baseline. No more raining keyboards or choral brooms. Beans on toast would suffice. I simmered pinto beans, tasting cumin and smoke the way time indexes memory inside legumes. They sang small calorie hymns in a minor pentatonic scale. As I ate, every bite slipped a metaphor onto the table: this spoon was the moon raking tides; those beans the recalcitrant minutes trying to coagulate. I took memos, chewing epiphany. Hunger quieted into satiety, which is just potential energy resting before its gymnastic reincarnation.

Then came afternoon—that amnesiac raconteur. Afternoon does not believe in its own existence until it has been archived by coffee and pigeons and laundromat cyclic dryers. I walked to the corner store and in doing so re-remembered that even sidewalks gossip in syllables of gum. Each old stain memorializes a mouth that chewed expectancy until brittle, then ejected expectation onto progress. City planners call it bio-residue. Poets call it longitude of longing indulged. I call it Tuesday residue painted flat by feet whose soles never noticed they were canvases.

At the store I bought bread thresholded through kneading histories—some wheat farmer in Saskatchewan also named Matthew now co-signs my purchase, though we’ve never met. Receipts always enlist strangers as witnesses. Three limes for two dollars carry the equatorial puckered grin of seven moons ago when the lime merchant’s daughter skipped stones across the irrigation canal en route to escape village drought. I added tomatoes whose redness once ripened at a traffic light stuck on stop. The cashier’s hands reminded me of winters spent shucking corn on grandma’s porch; I paid mostly in stories, coins secondary. Somewhere her scanner pinged a barcode sequence that translated into an aria from Tosca. The whole check-out line held its communal breath until C-minor resolved.

Returning home, I passed construction workers jackhammering rainbows straight through asphalt. They rotated palette wheel drills, shard storms turned prismatic. I nearly joined them, offering lime wedges to lubricate sky-bits, but realized I’d forgotten eggs. Another errand. On the detour I noticed a boy blowing bubbles from a want ad. Each bubble revealed prospective apartments available within bubble lifespan: three-to-four second leases expanded interior fantasies, collapsed, left vacancies for birds. In one popped bubble I registered my own studio reflected inverted—ceilings hosting furniture, teacups nesting on floors. The inversion felt truer than truth. Vertigo fizzed up through ankles, but delight fizzed higher.

I arrived back at four thirty. Dusk enrolled colors with greater diplomacy than I could muster after social exertions. My chairs welcomed my hips like accustomed museums welcome frequent visitors. I settled to disassemble a lamp whose anxiety bulb had blinked. Unscrewing the globe disclosed mothscale galaxies that transformed kitchen dark into nebula soup. I soldered no wires; memory did the conducting. The lamp brightened itself without electricity, proof that attention can override physics when attention chooses. Light pooled amber across dictionary pages I’d torn out years back during fits of definition divorces. Now, corralled by lamplight, words reunited unprompted: “benison” paired with “undone,” “microcosm” learned salsa with “macrocarpa.” They twirled across the floor in shoals like silverfish. I watched without referee: language deserves consensual choreography.

At last evening demanded accounting. I recognized my two feet still anchored to same kitchen where sodium streetlight first derailed default daylight. Yet they were rinsed in star-orchard light now, mapped with alleycat tobacco laughs and Russian violet fragrance and quartz dust from the train that never arrives timely according to maps. I walked barefoot to the stove, heated milk, misread desire for hot chocolate but allowed hope to override and thus invented midnight pudding from powdered starlight and lactose. It tasted sequentially of cinnamon, contrails, cardboard confession boxes, then finally of the cocoa I initially lacked. Absence was merely flavor relocation.

I carried the pudding to balcony just as visible stars peeked through city haze. Somewhere the wolves I’d trekked with paced terraces across the constellations, seeking familiar smells. I clicked tongue thrice—wolf dialect for keep walking. They signed back via constellation twinkles: — — — / · — · good dream realized. I measured my heartbeat against theirs; 64 bpm averaged across interstellar void. While spooning pudding, I recounted to them (knowing sound travels slowly across light-years) the news: alarms silenced, orchards safe, receipts honored, beans digested, lamplight consensual. I could not see if they nodded—their silhouettes subtended by about three degrees of arc—but a meteor shower shot across Cygnus in approval. One meteor carved an ampersand between hearts distant by heaven’s standard accounting, so maybe constellations are also grammarians of implied connection.

Between the rails of the meteor’s remaining stardust I wrote an unwritten letter that begins, ends, and resolves inside this very moment—with no postal price required except attention paid in full. To whom it may concern: The night folds inside your eyelid the way umbrellas fold; when opened skyward, entire symphonies of rain shower merely ourselves disguised. The message is happy to wait in marrow closets until you too wake at four fifteen by a flickering bulb orchestrating time into cathedral choirs.

Somewhere the sodium lamp exhaled a final flare, then ceded authority to planetand then orbit, and my pupils adjusted to receive the gradual gift of blue. The light had stolen nothing; it traded transparency for transparency, ushering inside what was already indoors: the interlaced epochs simmering in teakettle memory, the postal catastrophes arriving disguised as seasons, the child still holding the same blue balloon except now the balloon englobes the Milky Way while the child remains as bright and unsure as ever. I tiptoed back to bed, careful not to disturb my own footprints still spinning freshly minted pentimenti across the floorboards.

As sleep approached, the walls resumed their normal thickness. Yet a slender thread of orchard dusk looped round my finger, gentle reminder that thickness is a courtesy, not a verdict. Whether I remember tomorrow’s physical to-do list—license tabs, defrosted meat, email backlog—matters less than remembering the thread. It will tug whenever I risk defaulting to prepackaged linearity. I rolled onto my side. The mattress took custody of breathing context. Beyond, city transistor radios switched to predawn traffic. Out there, strangers negotiated their own retrievals from lost and found cosmos. I slept. The house exhaled a single copious sigh in relief that its dreamer had re-merged with circadian consensus. Streetlight blinked one last off-duty flicker—half wink, half benison—then recycled itself into elsewhere diodes.

And if you’ve read this far through the spirals and sine waves of episode, welcome to whatever section of night is yours: dawn in some archipelago, dusk in another. The particles stroking your retina once dwelled inside supernova lungs and poeticilium fields. They have taken roadside detours via dreams, detours whose mileage is tallied only by patience. Carry whichever scraps resonate—only remember to lie gently to gravity when you answer, “I’m staying put.” Because you’re moving on tectonic poetry plates at three centimeters a year—hardly noticeable until continents kiss. Until then, keep an ember of orchard warmth on the inside of your eyelids. May whatever house dreams you tonight compost sour old narratives into fertile loam for tentative, looping, luminous tomorrow.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
